text.title: MLP Four-Layer Framework
text.summary: Defines the four-layer MLP model, MVP vs MLP comparison, five delight categories, polish priority formula, and five-step planning process for evolving a viable product into a lovable one.

rule.statement: All core features must score 4+ on all four layers before claiming MLP status. Delight (Layer 4) cannot compensate for gaps in Functional (1), Reliable (2), or Usable (3).
rule.rationale: Delight applied to a broken or confusing product amplifies the frustration — users notice the personality of a product that still crashes or loses their data more acutely than they would on a dull product.

list.item: Layer 1 — Functional: core job gets done, no blocking bugs, acceptable performance.
Layer 2 — Reliable: consistent experience, error handling, edge cases covered, works as expected every time.
Layer 3 — Usable: easy to learn, efficient to use, minimal friction, good defaults.
Layer 4 — Delightful: emotional satisfaction, surprising moments, premium feel, shareable moments.
MVP goal: validate. MLP goal: retain and generate word of mouth.
MVP focus: functionality. MLP focus: experience on top of that functionality.
MVP emotion: "it works." MLP emotion: "I love it."
MVP word of mouth: unlikely. MLP word of mouth: likely.

rule.statement: MLP = MVP + delight. Not more features — better experience of existing features. Audit before building; most products reach MLP by polishing the core, not by adding functionality.
rule.rationale: Adding features to a sub-MLP product increases surface area for friction without addressing the root cause: the existing interactions are not good enough to generate retention.

list.item: Speed: faster than expected — instant search results, sub-100ms interactions.
Simplicity: easier than expected — one-click checkout, zero-configuration defaults.
Personality: unexpected charm — clever copywriting, brand-consistent micro-copy.
Anticipation: knows what users need before they ask — smart defaults, pre-filled fields.
Celebration: acknowledges user success — confetti on completion, "you've earned $X" summaries.

rule.statement: Require at least one delight idea per category from the audit before picking the sprint focus. Agents default to Personality and Celebration; Speed and Anticipation are underrepresented without forcing.
rule.rationale: Speed is the cheapest and most underrated delight lever — performance budgets on the core action produce retention lift with zero UX redesign cost. Forcing coverage prevents category tunnel-vision.

rule.statement: Score each polish task as Pain x Frequency x Visibility. If any factor is hard to estimate, use additive scoring (Pain + Frequency + Visibility) to avoid a single zero zeroing out a real opportunity.
rule.rationale: The multiplicative formula breaks down when any dimension is genuinely uncertain — a low-frequency interaction that causes severe pain still deserves consideration, but a zero in Frequency produces a score of zero multiplicatively.

list.item: Step 1 — Audit: score each core feature 1-5 on all four layers using current user verbatims and session replay as evidence. Do not score against aspirations.
Step 2 — Identify opportunities: cluster negative-but-not-blocking feedback ("works but underwhelming") by delight category.
Step 3 — Prioritize: rank by Pain x Frequency x Visibility, max 12 items.
Step 4 — Define MLP criteria: write numeric completion criteria before starting (e.g., Day-30 retention target, NPS target, core action time budget).
Step 5 — Plan the gap: build a sprint-by-sprint polish backlog, time-box each delight sprint at 2 weeks maximum.

text.reference: MLP planning document with feature audit, delight opportunities, polish backlog, completion criteria, and measurement plan.
Bash: converts mlp-gap-finder JSON into Linear issues with Pain x Freq x Vis priority.
